contaminant.name: Aerospace Sealant Residue
contaminant.description: Aviation sealant contamination arises when excess polysulfide or silicone materials, applied to seal aircraft joints, cure unevenly and harden into a rubbery residue during assembly. It stands out from oils or paints through its elastic, crosslinked polymer structure that resists basic solvents and wipes, turning tenacious on surfaces. On aluminum and titanium, it bonds deeply into oxide layers and pores, while infiltrating resin matrices in carbon fiber reinforced polymer and strands in fiberglass, creating stress points; removal challenges traditional scraping or chemicals, which risk damage and prolong downtime. Laser cleaning works out effectively by pulsing targeted energy to vaporize layers precisely, clearing residues without abrading substrates and preserving structural integrity across these materials.

fireExplosionRisk.description: Minimal fire risk with standard precautions and adequate ventilation
fireExplosionRisk.mitigation: Standard fire safety precautions, extinguisher available within 15m

toxicGasRisk.description: Multiple toxic compounds detected: Formaldehyde, Carbon Monoxide, Particulate Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons - requires enhanced protection
toxicGasRisk.mitigation: Half-face or full-face respirator with organic vapor/particulate cartridges, adequate ventilation. WARNING: Formaldehyde, Particulate Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons - known carcinogen(s), minimize exposure

visibilityHazard.description: Moderate visibility reduction (40-60%), significant particulate haze
visibilityHazard.mitigation: Ensure clear sight lines, use source extraction, maintain awareness of surroundings

ventilationRequirements.rationale: Standard industrial ventilation (12 ACH) for particulate control
